words: SAM SCHRAGER; Let me ask you some things. The first thing— we talked about
it a little last time, but I didn't put it down on the tape at all— and
that was, you described how you went about clearing the land, and the
problems in clearing.
CARL OLSON: Oh, well, the way they cleared the land, y'know, there was all
kinds of heavy timber in here. And the big stuff they couldn't do any
thing with, so they ringed the trees so they'd die, y'see. And then,
they cut the tree down, and then they'd bore holes in it, one this way
and one that way. And then they dropped hot coals in there, and they
had a little bellows there to get the fire started, and then she started
inside, that tree would burn up, y'see. That's the only way they could
get rid of it, they had no way to move those trees. And the other systems
they used, too. The same with a stump: they'd bore a hole there and one
more here, so they'd get a smokestack, you see, and it would lay there
and burn by itself, get rid of the rest. Then when it come to clear the
land, then you had to break it with a plow, breaking plow, y'know, hold
the handles. And brush, when you'd hit the brush the horses would stop,
(chuckles) couldn't make it. You had to break the plow loose, and take
your axe out, and get up there and chop them roots off so the knife could
get through y'know. There was a knife on the breaking plow that cut,
y'see, when the roots was too big they wouldn't cut 'em, so they had to
cut 'em off with a knife, see. And that's the way they broke the land
up. That was a slow process. If you'd break half an acre a day, that
was plenty, maybe, because you had to fight all that stuff. And then
you wasn't through. Then you had to let it lay over winter, so the sod
kind of rotted, a little bit, and got loose, and then you had to go out
there and pull that brush by hand. You had a grub hoe with you, see.
And when they got to a big brush you couldn't pull out, you had to grub
around it. So you get it out and you pile it up in the pile, see, and
then you keep on that way until you get it all. And then you go back
and burn all the brush afterwards, set fire to those bunches, y'see.
That's the way they had to clear land. So you know how much work that
was. Now they take a bulldozer and push that thing off in a day. (chuckles)
So you see what they had to go through with here, that people don't under
stand, y'know, at all, these new people, what a big job it was to clear
an acre. That was a big job if you had a lot of brush on it and stuff,
y'know. Then you had to cut the brush first, the big ones, and pile
them up and burn them, before you'd break, even.
Then you had the stumps to contend with, y'see. When you were break
ing there was a stump in your way, that'd throw your plow out and go
around the stump. But there was one guy here, (chuckles) he liked the
stumps on the hill. He lived out here. And he wouldn't even set fire
on one, so it would burn, y'know, he kept plowing and plowing and plow
ing; and when the stump was here the dirt would fall over and leave a
big hole below the stump, y'see, because it didn't throw it over enough.
So one day a feller come up by there, I was working for this guy, "I
seen the old man standing there with a stick today," he said, "putting
the brace against thatftump, and he was crying. So, I stopped and
went out there," he said, "and asked him what's the matter. ' Oh, this
stump is falling over,' he said, 'I don't want it to fall over!'"(laugh
ing^) That's true too, y'know. Well, it wasn't, y'know, exactly all
the truth, y'know, but he never touched them. Those stumps'd been there
so long that maybe you could kick 'em out, y'know, they were rotten.
But he wouldn't monkey with them, he plowed around them, see. And was
apretty steep sidehill, like this, where they were. Iseen the stumps
there all the time when I drove by. (Laughs) So you can see a lot of
funny things too, y'know.

speaker: SAM
words: Did he just like the stumps, was he lazy?

speaker: CO
words: Well, I don't know. He was kind of lazy all right. He just got by, y'

speaker: CO
words: know, he was one of these farmers that done just so much, is all. When

speaker: CO
words: he built his house, why, he didn't put the corners on 'em or nothing,

speaker: CO
words: y'know. Boards were sticking out like this, he didn't saw them off even.

speaker: CO
words: That's the way his house looked. And everybody was talking about it:

speaker: CO
words: when he come to a corner, like this, y'know, and then a board would be

speaker: CO
words: too long, he wouldn't even saw it off, he let it stick out there instead

speaker: CO
words: of put the corner on it. So the whole house burnt up one time, and he

speaker: CO
words: built a new one, and he put no corners on that one either. (Laughs) And

speaker: CO
words: you drive right by it when you go to Deary, about a mile out of town here.

speaker: CO
words: Yep, there was a lot of other things going on in those days.

speaker: SS
words: Well, hoitfvould you get the stumps out mostly?

speaker: CO
words: Well, after a while, y'know, then they had stump pullers. You had to

speaker: CO
words: have drums, see. They made it themselves, the blacksmith, y'know. They

speaker: CO
words: can make a drum, and then they have a sweep on it, and then a horse on

speaker: CO
words: the end there, y'see. And then they put a cable around the stump, and

speaker: CO
words: then the horse would go and wrap that cable around and pull the stump

speaker: CO
words: out. But, you coulnd't take very big stumps with that, y know, maybe

speaker: CO
words: like, but bigger than that they couldn't pull them out at all, y'know.

speaker: CO
words: So you couldn't get 'em all, you had to burn the big ones. But the stumps

speaker: CO
words: that was about a fotffc through or smaller, you could pull them out with

speaker: CO
words: the stump puller.

speaker: SS
words: Did you use dynamite at all?

speaker: CO
words: Well, you coulr^'t buy any dynamite around here then, it was too high

speaker: CO
words: of price for one thing, the people didn't have it, and it was dangerous

speaker: CO
words: to have, y know. you have kids around and have dynamite, y'know, that's

speaker: CO
words: no good. That's pretty dangerous stuff. But I cleared up some of my

speaker: CO
words: land, I used dynamite on that. That was after the First World War,.

speaker: CO
words: They had some cheap powder they sold then that worked pretty good for

speaker: CO
words: stumps. It wasn't so strong, y'know. If you get too strong a powder

speaker: CO
words: under the stump, it cracks it instead of pushing it out, y'know, so you'd

speaker: CO
words: have a sliver sitting there. But this powder the government had didn't

speaker: CO
words: have that much power, so it'd pull the stump out pretty good, y'see. So

speaker: CO
words: I used some of that. Yup.

speaker: SS
words: How would you set that up to blow out the stumps with the dynamite?

speaker: CO
words: Well, we'd just make a hole under it and stick a stick in, and put a fuse

speaker: CO
words: on it and light it, and bang, she'd go, see. You had to get away from

speaker: CO
words: it, the old stump'd be flying in the air. I told you that when I was

speaker: CO
words: plowing how I got rid of the roots. Yeah, yeah, I did. I guess you for

speaker: CO
words: got about it.

speaker: SS
words: Tell me again.

speaker: CO
words: Well, I tied a sack of dynamite sticks on the handle of the plow, and

speaker: CO
words: whenever I hit a stump I stopped and stuck it in, about a half a stick

speaker: CO
words: was enough to blow that out. And then I'd tell the horses to "get up"

speaker: CO
words: and bang (chuckles) , I'd be sure the horses went fast enough so that

speaker: CO
words: it wouldn't blow up too fast. So, that's the way I got all of the stumps

speaker: CO
words: out. That was, the roots, y'know, you couldn't see them. You'd hit that,

speaker: CO
words: and kind of mean to farm, y'know. When you got all the stumps out there

speaker: CO
words: was nothing to it.

speaker: SS
words: What was the main diet that the family had on their homestead? What

speaker: SS
words: did you eat when you were a kid, mostly?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, now, well, I'll tell you. Skim milk, and mush, and potatoes, and

speaker: CO
words: if you had any fruit, you'd eat that.' That took a while to get the fruit

speaker: CO
words: trees up, y'know, so you didn't have that right away. But we had a pretty

speaker: CO
words: good sized orchard on our place. That's what we ate, about all, and a

speaker: CO
words: little meat, y'know. We had no refrigerators. You couldn't go out and

speaker: CO
words: butcher a steer or anything in the. middle of the summer, it d^spjil, see.

speaker: CO
words: So you never got any meat. But most of them, when they'd been here a

speaker: CO
words: while, they went together and butchered a cow or something, y'know; and

speaker: CO
words: then they'd take a quarter apiece home, see, and it would keep in the

speaker: CO
words: winter because it was cold. That's the only time you had beef, would

speaker: CO
words: be in the winter. And in the summer you had pork— we salted that down

speaker: CO
words: in a barrel, see, salt pork. The pork keeps pretty good, y'know. You

speaker: CO
words: salt meat down pretty good too, but it's got to be awful strong brine

speaker: CO
words: then on it, y'know. So that's what we had to eat then— pork in the sum

speaker: CO
words: mertime and meat in the winter mostly.

speaker: SS
words: What aid you tell me last time about how often you had much?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, every night we had it, pretty near. Graham look a sack of grain and

speaker: CO
words: grind it on that watermill they had up there, y'know. Big stones that

speaker: CO
words: went around. Cost you two bits a sack to grind it. That's 150 pounds,

speaker: CO
words: about. Then we cooked mush out of that, see, we'd get all the grain

speaker: CO
words: that way. It was healthy food, y'know. So, you see it didn't cost much

speaker: CO
words: to feed a family in those days. A sack of mush like that would last our

speaker: CO
words: family all winter, and that's only about a dollar's worth. (Chuckles)

speaker: CO
words: It'fl cost you more'fdior a sandwich now!

speaker: SS
words: Would you have anything else for supper besides the mush, or would that

speaker: SS
words: be it?

speaker: CO
words: Well, we drank skim milk with it, and bread, if you want to eat some bread,

speaker: CO
words: but it's no use to eat any bread with it because you had your bread in

speaker: CO
words: the mush, y'see. And that's what we had for supper pret'near all the time,

speaker: CO
words: that is, the first fifteen, twenty years, y'know. After that, after you

speaker: CO
words: got more field and more work, then you had a little more money, so you

speaker: CO
words: could buy some stuff once in a while. But we never seen an orange or

speaker: CO
words: anything like that, no. We'd get some hard candy once in awhile when

speaker: CO
words: Dad went down and bought a little groceries. The old clerk, he'd always

speaker: CO
words: stick in a package of candy in because he knew we had a lot of kids. So

speaker: CO
words: we got one apiece and then they were gone. (Chuckles),. Yep, that

speaker: CO
words: was homesteading.

speaker: CO
words: Then, the water problem— we got water problems now, but they had

speaker: CO
words: water problems worse than these. See, unless you happened to have a place

speaker: CO
words: where it was a natural spring. But if there was no water around, yjHere,

speaker: CO
words: are you going to get it? And the trouble with the homesteaders here,

speaker: CO
words: they just stayed in Minnesota, a lot of them, and there the soil was sandy.

speaker: CO
words: And you couldn't dig a well, it wouldn't hold it, y'see, it'd leak out.

speaker: CO
words: So they thought it would be the same here, so they never dug no wells the

speaker: CO
words: first few years. But here they found out that it was hard pan under hereyou

speaker: CO
words: know what that is, kind of a clay, and that's waterproof. So my dad,

speaker: CO
words: he made a cistern twelve feet wide and about eight foot deep in that hardpan,

speaker: CO
words: and then he set it with br'r ck. And that's the water we had, y'see.

speaker: CO
words: No more. We couldn't waste any water there. I betcha for all the kids

speaker: CO
words: that was home, we didn't use up five gallons a day!! And to start with,

speaker: CO
words: my mother carried water for half a mile in the bucket, half a mile to the

speaker: CO
words: spring, see, before they knew they could make a cistern. And you couldn't

speaker: CO
words: make acistern'over that that would hold either.f you hit the wrong kind

speaker: CO
words: of a clay, it wouldn't hold, and if you hit soapstone, they call it, that

speaker: CO
words: would leak out, like that. You had to hit hardpan, real hardpan, to hold

speaker: CO
words: it. But it would hold just like a real tank y'know. It wouldn't leak

speaker: CO
words: at all, just waterproof. I remember when my dad was making a big cistern.

speaker: CO
words: A butcher from Troy come up here, y'know, to buy a cow, and he looked

speaker: CO
words: at that cistern— he thought that, was great. And he told about how many

speaker: CO
words: gallon it would hold, too, I remember that. Yah.

speaker: SS
words: He told about how many gallons, your father did?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, I forget how many he said now. It'd be about a thousand gallons,

speaker: CO
words: maybe. Yeah, something like that.

speaker: SS
words: Would it come close to drying up in the summer?

speaker: CO
words: No, it wouldn't dry up at all. You see the upper part of it was topsoil,

speaker: CO
words: that wouldn't hold it, y'know. So, you had about three or four feet in

speaker: CO
words: at the top there that wouldn't hold the water at all. It was down below

speaker: CO
words: where you hit the hardpan; that'd hold good, y'know. And then if it rain

speaker: CO
words: ed it'll seep in a little too on the sides, y'see. Yup, the old cistern,

speaker: CO
words: I remember that. And then we had the pump, hand pump on it. That was

speaker: CO
words: good water, though. You could see there was no mineral in it or nothing,

speaker: CO
words: because it was rainwater, all of it. And then you'd catch water from

speaker: CO
words: the eaves too in barrels, y'know, for washing clothes with. That was

speaker: CO
words: no good to drink, because there'd be soot on the roof, y'know, and the

speaker: CO
words: water'd get black, kind of. So that was the water problem. You see, it

speaker: CO
words: was just as bad as Troy got it now. (chuckles). They're just about run

speaker: CO
words: ning out here.

speaker: SS
words: How could you make five gallons go for the whole family a day?

speaker: CO
words: Well, when we washed, we all washed in the same one. You'd never throw

speaker: CO
words: the water out, see. And you washed clothes, you had to wash in the same

speaker: CO
words: thing. Of course, we didn't take very many baths. My dad, he made a'tub

speaker: CO
words: of wood, see.we'd all bathe in the same water, see. "Tee", they

speaker: CO
words: called in Swede, that tub. I remember that. It was about that deep, and

speaker: CO
words: it was made of wood, y'know; sitting up and down this way, and then iron

speaker: CO
words: straps around to hold it together.

speaker: SS
words: It's about three feet deep?

speaker: CO
words: Yea, about that. Yup, and, oh, it'd be about six feet long maybe, and

speaker: CO
words: two and one half feet wide. That was sitting out on the ground, y know,

speaker: CO
words: you wouldn't have no room in the house for that. So in the winter, you

speaker: CO
words: didn't take any_baths. So maybe, that's good, too, (cjmckle^ because I

speaker: CO
words: read about two, three years ago in the paper where the doctoraexamining

speaker: CO
words: why people get cancer so much. They found out the people that take the

speaker: CO
words: most baths had the most cancer. Do you believe that?

speaker: SS
words: I don't know.

speaker: CO
words: Yeah, that's what they elate. Iseen some more about it lately too,

speaker: CO
words: somebody wrote about the same article. They claim your skin cleans it

speaker: CO
words: self, and it's achemical or something that comes out here and stops the

speaker: CO
words: germs from going in your system, see. And long ago you hardly ever

speaker: CO
words: heard of any cancer at all, y'know, and now it's just all kinds of it in

speaker: CO
words: the country. Idon't know. And the doctor that wrote this, he says,"I

speaker: CO
words: hate to write this, it sounds bad, because people won't take abath maybe.

speaker: SS
words: Can you explain how the trails grew? Did each homestead make it own

speaker: SS
words: trail to its own place?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, trails. It wasn't too thick, timber, y'know. You didn't have to

speaker: CO
words: make any trails if you had some cows. They made the trails for you.

speaker: CO
words: When they went to the pasture they'd take the same route all the time.

speaker: CO
words: There's where we had our coyote traps, in them cow trails, y'know. They

speaker: CO
words: were all over. We had one that we called the big trail, because the cows

speaker: CO
words: went to that part of the canyon for pasture, because it was better past

speaker: CO
words: ure, and they'd wear it down, Hitt deep, y'know, in asidehSU- But on

speaker: CO
words: the level they didn't wear 'em down very far, and it'd be about this wide,

speaker: CO
words: y'know. Cows always made atrail, and that's the kind of trails we had

speaker: CO
words: on the ranch. But if you had to pack something in, then you had to chop

speaker: CO
words: atrail, y'know, and make it wide enough to carry the pack, for the hor

speaker: CO
words: ses, y'know. But the other trails were cow trails. Then everybody had

speaker: CO
words: cattle for milk, y'see.

speaker: SS
words: What about the trail to the homestead itself, to get into your own place

speaker: SS
words: and to the house? Did you just cut that in?

speaker: CO
words: Oh yeah, you didn't have a trail there. You had awagon road in, they

speaker: CO
words: called it. See, you'd cut out wide enough for the wagons, about that

speaker: CO
words: wide, see, and you'd cut out that much brush 1± the brush was too heavy,

speaker: CO
words: and sawed off the stumps close to the ground. You see, if you stuck 'em

speaker: CO
words: up, the wagon wheels or the axle would hit them. So that's the way

speaker: CO
words: they all made those trails. There's alot of those roads all over, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: because after they'd been here for afew years, they all had a team, y'

speaker: CO
words: know, or horses, because they had that to farm with. So they all had a

speaker: CO
words: wagon road in, y'know. And it wasn't like it is having automobiles now,

speaker: CO
words: y'know. You know what the two fellers said when they met on the road?

speaker: CO
words: A feller was coming with a load of wood or something, and he. met the other

speaker: CO
words: feller, "If you don't get out of my way Iwill do the same that I done to

speaker: CO
words: the other ...guy. I met!" "What did you do?" "I drove around him!" You

speaker: CO
words: can't do that when you meet a car now. You'll bang, like that (claps

speaker: CO
words: his hands)

speaker: SS
words: How did they decide where the main wagon roads went, that went from town

speaker: SS
words: to town?

speaker: CO
words: Yeah, that was the county that done that. And that's why they had so

speaker: CO
words: many hills in it, y'know, because they followed the lines between the

speaker: CO
words: places. They wouldn't go across unless they had to, y'see. So we had

speaker: CO
words: hills like this, y'know, all over the country. They followed survey

speaker: CO
words: lines between. If your place were over here and mine here, the road would

speaker: CO
words: be here, see.

speaker: SS
words: Right on the line.

speaker: CO
words: Yeah, on the line, see. That's why we had so steep roads to start with.

speaker: CO
words: You wouldn't think about going across the other man's place. No. You'd

speaker: CO
words: go up and down, up and down, y'know, like that. They didn't have no auto

speaker: CO
words: mobiles then, anyway, they had horses to pull over the hills. But when

speaker: CO
words: we come to haul something, then -you were in a pickle if you had too many

speaker: CO
words: hills, y'know, you couldn't haul much of a load. We had a lot of them.

speaker: CO
words: That wasn't all, we had the mud to contend with! That was the worst.

speaker: CO
words: You know how big a wagon wheel is? And the hub was about that far from

speaker: CO
words: the ground, and we had a lot of places where we had mud that went clean

speaker: CO
words: up to the axle in the spring. So it was a heck of a job to get to town

speaker: CO
words: in the spring, y'know. The tires were narrow, and the wheels, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: they'd cut and cut and cut and the water would stay in them. There was

speaker: CO
words: no grade or nothing, see. So the roads were terrible, y'know. It's no

speaker: CO
words: thing like it is now. That's why where we lived, y'know, we were nine

speaker: CO
words: miles from town, so we couldn't cut any cordwood or ties and haul them

speaker: CO
words: We couldn't make it in a day. See, that made it a little tougher.

speaker: CO
words: The people that lived within two or three or four miles, y'know, they

speaker: CO
words: could haul their stuff in. But we had three or four bills a quarter

speaker: CO
words: of a mile long and about that steep, y'know how long it takes to get up

speaker: CO
words: there with a team of horses and a load. You had to rest them horses may

speaker: CO
words: be ten times before you got over that hill.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a grade do you think that would be?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, about twenty percent, anyway. You see, we couldn't change them,

speaker: CO
words: there's no way to change them hardly. There was too much timber maybe,

speaker: CO
words: and so forth, or you get on the other man's place, and they wouldn't do

speaker: CO
words: that. But some places, you had to, because there was no other way to

speaker: CO
words: get around it, then you had to go on the man's place. It was too steep

speaker: CO
words: and too much timber, y'know, big trees and thick, you couldn't build no

speaker: CO
words: roads there at all. The big hill we had from our place was the Dry Creek

speaker: CO
words: Hill, it was a creek they called Dry Creek, and they had the bridge there

speaker: CO
words: about oh, seventy-five feet long and about thirty-forty feet high. See,

speaker: CO
words: that was quite a bridge to build in those days, because it was down like

speaker: CO
words: this and then up like that, y'know. I crossed on that bridge with my

speaker: CO
words: thrashing outfit, I was scared to death every time, I thought it might

speaker: CO
words: go down. So we'd take the engine off, and then we had a long cable that

speaker: CO
words: we put on the thrashing machine so we wouldn't get all the weight on one

speaker: CO
words: spot— that's the way we crossed the bridge all the time, see. But there's

speaker: CO
words: no road there no more now. They took it around.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know who built that bridge or bridges like that?

speaker: CO
words: A feller by the name of Ely. Ely, he was a bridge builder. There was

speaker: CO
words: three or four of 'em, he said. One of them lived on Little Bear.

speaker: SS
words: Would the county pay for that or would the homesteaders?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, yeah, if it was a county road, they would, yeah, but if you didn't

speaker: CO
words: have a county road, then you had to take care of it yourself, see. Then

speaker: CO
words: they had the poll tax, those days they called it, y'know. Two dollars

speaker: CO
words: I think it was, poll tax. Everybody had to pay that tax when he was twe

speaker: CO
words: nty-one years old. Then you could work it out if you wanted instead of

speaker: CO
words: paying the two dollars, so they worked it out on the roads, see. That's

speaker: CO
words: the way they kept up the roads, quite a bit, just from the poll tax alone.

speaker: CO
words: I even worked out the poll tax myself.

speaker: SS
words: Did the poll tax give you the right to vote? Is that it?

speaker: CO
words: Oh, yeah, you had to be twenty-one.' The poll tax was put in more for

speaker: CO
words: building roads than anything else, y'see. See, and then they started

speaker: CO
words: getting machinery and they took off the poll tax. You don't pay

speaker: CO
words: that more now. They had that all over the United States, I guess,

speaker: CO
words: at one time.

speaker: SS
words: Was the road from Troy, let's say, out to your place, already there when

speaker: SS
words: your folks started homesteading?

speaker: CO
words: No, there was no roads at all. From Troy east was no road whatever, no

speaker: CO
words: road anywhere, not even a trail! Because it'd just been surveyed, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: ready for homesteading. Nobody lived there, y'see, it was wild country.

speaker: CO
words: But y'see. when they keep moving in, moving in, they went between the

speaker: CO
words: trees, wherever they could with their loads. And some of them, they'd

speaker: CO
words: fool around a couple of years before they had any road at all. They'd

speaker: CO
words: carry it on their backs, see. Suppose a road went here, now you were a

speaker: CO
words: half a mile from the road, he'd walk in from his place out to the road,

speaker: CO

speaker: CO
words: Oh yeah, yeah, sure. Yep.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me some of the stories about liora, about the town of Nora?

speaker: CO
words: Well, I don't know, I think I told you all of them, that time.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, but you — we didn't put any of them down on the tape, and that's

speaker: SS
words: what I wanted to get.

speaker: CO
words: About the pennies, and the boy and the pennies? Well, the boy come in,

speaker: CO
words: and the reason I get so much kick out of it now is because they're still

speaker: CO
words: using pennies, and I don't see why they use pennies today. Well, he come

speaker: CO
words: in with a handful of pennies, maybe had six, seven, eight, of them. Want

speaker: CO
words: ed some candy from the boss, and the clerk said, "Go on, throw them in

speaker: CO
words: the creek, and I'll give you the candy!" And the kid done that. They

speaker: CO
words: wouldn't handle any pennies, they didn't want no pennies those days. If

speaker: CO
words: you owed the man. if you figured you owe two and a half you paid him a

speaker: CO
words: nickel, or the other way, see. They didn't want to monkey with pennies.

speaker: CO
words: And today they put pennies on an automobile, even. (Laughs) . That s

speaker: CO
words: Ahwhy I get a kick out of it today because they're using pennies, and

speaker: CO
words: a penny was worth as much as a dollar is now, in those days, yeah?

speaker: CO
words: cording to what you get for 'em. I guess I told you about the bra. You

speaker: CO
words: don't want that on there?

speaker: SS
words: Yah, I do, that's a good story, the bra.

speaker: CO
words: (chuckles) Well, they had a bra laying in the store there. It was P.E.

speaker: CO
words: Johnson, I knew him, he was neighbors of ours, and he was in the store

speaker: CO
words: then—and B.S. Nilson— he was a photographer, he run all around and took

speaker: CO
words: pictures all the time, and he was in the store too then. They owned

speaker: CO
words: it together. And that bra was laying there for years, y'know, and they

speaker: CO
words: couldn't sell it. So when Christmas come (chuckles) they wrapped it up

speaker: CO
words: and sent it to a girl. And the girl never said anything, y'know, until

speaker: CO
words: next Christmas, and then she took a horse turd and wrapped it up and wrote,

speaker: CO
words: "B.S. Nelson and P. E. Johnson, be so kind and divide this between you!"

speaker: CO
words: (Laughs) She knew where it come from, 'cause you seen it in the showcase

speaker: CO
words: all the time. Yup, and that's no story, that's true, absolutely true,

speaker: CO
words: the whole thing. ^oy everything I've told you is true. I don't stretch

speaker: CO
words: nothing. It all happened. My uncle he had that store down at Nora five,

speaker: CO
words: six years, and I stayed with him down there. But this was before my uncle

speaker: CO
words: had it. My uncle had it there on a Halloween night. The Halloweens come

speaker: CO
words: up, and he had kind of a storehouse in the back of the store, y'know, with

speaker: CO
words: a roof, y'know, on like that. And here come the Halloweens, and there was

speaker: CO
words: a buggy set in there. And he went and hid, he didn't show himself, my

speaker: CO
words: undertook and put two planks up, y'know, and then they took the buggy,

speaker: CO
words: and they put a rope on it, and they pulled it clean on top of that build

speaker: CO
words: ing, see, and on up to the peak. They set the buggy this way, see, and

speaker: CO
words: then they went down and when they got through my uncle come out and said,

speaker: CO
words: "Take down!". (Laughs) So they didn't get much out of that. Nora

speaker: CO
words: used to be quite a little town at one time, y'know. They had a saloon

speaker: CO
words: there, cigar factory, a blacksmith shop, and a hotel and a store right

speaker: CO
words: there, and a sawmill right there. Y'know. You know Patrick that pret'

speaker: CO
words: near lost his lip, y'know. He got drunk- 'course this is just a story,

speaker: CO
words: I suppose, but that's what they said. He got drunk and he fell off the

speaker: CO
words: wagon, and the wagon wheel hit him here and tore— this is true that

speaker: CO
words: the lip was tore off, all right. But they said he never knew his lip

speaker: CO
words: was tore off till he was gonna put a big chew of snoose in there, and

speaker: CO
words: there was no lip there. (Laughs)

speaker: SS
words: Was he one of the rough bunch?

speaker: CO
words: Well, he wasn't rough, he drank a lot though. He was drunk all the time,

speaker: CO
words: y'know, drank whiskey all the time. Kind of a nice guy, too. I knew

speaker: CO
words: him, seen him a few times. He froze to death, I believe, crossing the

speaker: CO
words: mountain here, carrying moonshine in the moonshine day^ if I ain't mis

speaker: CO
words: taken. Yup.

speaker: SS
words: What was this bunch like? You mentioned before that they really celebrated when

speaker: SS
words: Hays got killed.

speaker: CO
words: Oh, when they shot Hays? Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: What were they like? Was it just a bunch of guys that was drunk all the

speaker: SS
words: time, or what?

speaker: CO
words: Yeah, they drank, that Nora bunch there. They were known all over the

speaker: CO
words: country for being the biggest drinkers, y'know. They all drank, pret'

speaker: CO
words: near. Everybody up there they got drunk. They never bothered anybody

speaker: CO
words: though, but they were drinkers. And then when they shot Hays, y'know,

speaker: CO
words: they hated him, because I guess maybe he arrested some of them down here

speaker: CO
words: once in a while. So they celebrated for three weeks up there! Old Hays

speaker: CO
words: he was a mean guy,y y'know. He got a drunk guy and he'd tie a rope around

speaker: CO
words: his arms, and led him behind his horse just like he'd lead a cow. And

speaker: CO
words: then he arrested a guy that fell off a wagon because he was drunk. He

speaker: CO
words: had a lame back, y'know, and he missed a step, fell back, and here was

speaker: CO
words: Hays and grabbed him. (chuckles). And he throwed his own son in the

speaker: CO
words: jail another time.
